essence: The ultimate commodity-cycle business: capital-intensive extraction/processing where the global commodity price (exogenous) drives profit, and cost-per-tonne decides survival.
Position on the cost curve is everything - low-cost producers make money through the cycle, high-cost ones only at the peak.
Reserves/resource quality, scale, energy cost, and logistics are the core competitive position.
Highly cyclical (China demand is a swing factor), capital-heavy, and ESG/decarbonisation-pressured (steel/aluminium are emissions-heavy).
Battery/transition minerals (copper, lithium) are a structural growth theme; India is a major steel producer with import reliance in some minerals.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that extract, process, and sell metals and minerals - iron ore, steel, aluminium, copper, coal, and battery minerals.
definition.jobToBeDone: Supply the raw materials that build everything - construction, manufacturing, infrastructure, electronics, and the energy transition.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools go to low-cost-curve producers and to integrated players (mine-to-metal); commodity processing is cyclical and price-taking; value-added/specialty products and battery/transition minerals earn better, more defensible margins.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting toward battery/transition minerals (copper, lithium, nickel) and toward green metals (low-carbon steel/aluminium) as decarbonisation and electrification reshape demand; bulk commodity economics remain China-demand-driven.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per tonne of metal/ore sold
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Volume x Price. Volume = production/throughput; price = the global commodity price (largely exogenous, China-demand-driven) - so cost-per-tonne vs the price decides profit.
keyKPIs: Cost per tonne / position on cost curve

costStructure.shape: Huge upfront capex (mines, smelters) then variable costs dominated by energy, raw materials/ore, and logistics; profitability is the spread between cost-per-tonne and the commodity price - position on the cost curve is destiny.
costStructure.leverage: Extreme operating leverage to the commodity price - at the top of the cycle low-cost producers gush cash; at the bottom high-cost producers lose money. Energy cost is a make-or-break input (esp. aluminium).

competitionAndMoats.concentration: Global majors dominate bulk (BHP, Rio Tinto, Vale, Glencore); steel/aluminium have large regional players; India: Tata Steel, JSW Steel, Hindalco, Vedanta, Coal India (coal monopoly).

structuralForces.regulation: Heavy - mining licences/auctions, royalties, environmental & land/forest clearances, and increasingly carbon/ESG rules (carbon border taxes like CBAM threaten exports).
structuralForces.cyclicality: Among the most cyclical industries - driven by global commodity prices and especially Chinese demand and construction cycles.
structuralForces.substitution: Material substitution (aluminium vs steel, recycling/scrap vs primary), and recycling/circularity reduce primary demand.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Energy and coking-coal suppliers swing costs; equipment makers; large industrial buyers (autos, construction) and traders; governments control mineral access and royalties.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium - AI in exploration, mine operations, predictive maintenance, and process optimisation.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI lowers cost-per-tonne (the survival metric) via autonomous operations, predictive maintenance, and process/energy optimisation, and improves safety and recovery - sharpening cost-curve position without changing commodity-price exposure.
opportunities: Lower cost per tonne & higher recovery

ranges: Cost-curve position: the decisive survival metric (1st/2nd quartile = resilient)
EBITDA per tonne: swings hugely with the cycle
Capacity utilisation: high needed given fixed costs
Energy as % of cost: very high (aluminium especially)
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Move down the cost curve (energy, ore, efficiency)
Shift to value-added / specialty / transition minerals

keyTerms.Cost curve: Ranking of producers by cost-per-tonne - position decides who survives downturns.
keyTerms.Cost per tonne: All-in cost to produce a tonne of metal/ore.
keyTerms.LME: London Metal Exchange - sets benchmark metal prices.
keyTerms.Integrated (mine-to-metal): Owning the chain from ore to finished metal.
keyTerms.Scrap / secondary: Recycled metal - competes with primary production.
keyTerms.CBAM: Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism - EU carbon tariff on imports.

questionsToAskFirst: Where on the cost curve does it sit?
Cost per tonne vs the commodity price?
Balance-sheet strength to ride the cycle?

whatsUniqueHere: Metals & mining is the purest cost-curve game in business: the commodity price is set globally (above all by Chinese demand) and is utterly beyond any single company's control, so survival reduces to one question - are you a low-cost producer who makes money through the cycle, or a high-cost one who only earns at the peak? Reserves, energy, and integration set that position, and now decarbonisation adds a new axis: low-carbon green metal to avoid being penalised as the world prices carbon.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: India is the world's #2 steel producer and a major metals market: integrated steel majors (Tata Steel, JSW Steel, SAIL), aluminium/zinc (Hindalco, Vedanta), and a state coal monopoly (Coal India); strong domestic demand from infrastructure and construction, with import reliance in coking coal and some battery minerals.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Oil & gas - commodity, cyclical, asset-heavy, price-taking

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India metals: robust domestic steel demand from infrastructure; steelmakers expanding capacity; aluminium/zinc exposed to global prices and energy costs; decarbonisation (green steel, CBAM on EU exports) rising on the agenda; transition-mineral security (copper, lithium) a growing focus; China demand/oversupply the key swing factor.
